core_misconceptions: Assuming a small survey result represents the entire university population perfectly
Believing that increasing sample size always corrects for bias in who was asked
Thinking that inferential results are facts rather than probabilistic estimates
object.body_markdown: ## Talking about everyone vs. talking about some When a survey lands in your inbox, it is rarely sent to every single person at Harvard. Instead, researchers select a small slice of the community to speak for the whole. This is the difference between a population and a sample. A population is the entire group you want to understand, such as all undergraduates or all administrative staff. A sample is the subset you actually reach. Understanding this distinction is the bedrock of data fluency, as it changes whether you are describing an observed reality or making a guess about the wider world. ## The three components - The Population is your universe of interest. It includes every individual who meets your criteria, whether you have their data or not. - The Sample is the bridge to that universe. It is the limited set of people whose responses you have gathered to stand in for the rest. - The Inference is the leap of faith. It is the process of taking what you learned from your sample and projecting it onto the entire population. When these two groups are similar, your inference is strong. When your sample is narrow or skewed, the leap becomes risky. ## Why this matters for Harvard staff As staff, you are frequently asked to translate data into action. If you receive a report claiming that 'Harvard staff value remote work options,' you must immediately differentiate between the people who answered the survey and the entire staff population. Was the sample representative of every school and department, or did it skew heavily toward one campus? When you conflate the sample with the population, you risk designing policies or services that only address the needs of those who were most visible in your data, leaving the rest of the community behind. You do not need complex math to spot this; you only need to ask who is included in the set and who remains invisible. ## A reading example Consider this internal memo: 'Based on our recent survey of 400 faculty members, we can confirm that 80 percent of the faculty population supports the new research grant portal transition.' Before accepting this as a universal truth, see the language critically. The report conflates the 400 faculty members who responded (the sample) with the thousands of faculty across the University (the population). Did those 400 people represent every department, or just the ones who were already familiar with the portal? Thinking through the sampling method reveals that the 80 percent figure is an estimate of sentiment, not an absolute count of every faculty member's opinion. ## What to practice The next time you are presented with a survey or a data summary, use the See-Think-Wonder routine to ground your interpretation: 1. See: Look at the data provided. What does the report explicitly say about who participated in the study? 2. Think: Based on the participants, what do I think the limitations of this data are in representing the entire population? 3. Wonder: What questions do I have about the people who did not participate, and how might their input change the findings?
